children.name: Perca
children.description: True perch — the most abundant and widely distributed freshwater fish in Sweden and across the northern Holarctic. The European perch (abborre) is present in virtually every Swedish lake, from warm lowland waters to cold boreal lakes near the Arctic Circle. It is a bold, opportunistic predator with vertical dark stripes, a spiny first dorsal fin, and vivid red-orange ventral fins. Perch are strongly schooling fish in their early years, hunting invertebrates cooperatively; larger individuals become increasingly piscivorous and solitary. A perch angler's dream and a child's first catch across Scandinavia. The flesh is white, firm, and delicious — possibly the finest freshwater fish in Europe.

children.name: Perca fluviatilis
children.description: The most abundant fish in Swedish lakes (abborre). Almost certainly the first fish caught by Swedish children. Introduced to Australia and New Zealand where it is highly invasive. Can live to 22 years in slow-growing, cold populations. Striped pattern and red fins make it unmistakeable.

children.name: Perca flavescens
children.description: The North American counterpart of the European perch and equally popular with anglers. State fish of several US states. One of the most commercially valuable freshwater fish in North America. The Great Lakes support major commercial yellow perch fisheries.

children.name: Perca schrenkii
children.description: Endemic to Lake Balkhash and associated water bodies in Kazakhstan and Central Asia. Closely resembles the European perch but is restricted to the Balkhash–Alakol basin, where it is threatened by introduced predatory fish.

children.name: Perca granulata
children.description: A recently identified cryptic species from Patagonian lakes; fine granular scale texture.

children.name: Perca erythrinus
children.description: A species of Perca found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Perca lankaensis
children.description: A species of Perca found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Perca cubensis
children.description: A species of Perca found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Perca concavus
children.description: A species of Perca found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Perca virginianus
children.description: A species of Perca found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Perca discifer
children.description: A species of Perca found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Perca densatus
children.description: A species of Perca found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Perca cornatus
children.description: A species of Perca found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Perca splendens
children.description: A species of Perca found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Perca aequatorialis
children.description: A species of Perca found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Sander
children.description: Zander and walleye — large, predatory perch relatives with glassy, light-gathering eyes that allow them to hunt effectively in turbid and low-light conditions. The zander (gös) is Sweden's most sought-after table fish — its white, boneless flesh is considered superior to any other freshwater fish by many Swedish anglers. Zander are the 'wolves of freshwater', highly social pack hunters that drive shoals of prey fish to the surface in coordinated attacks. They have expanded their range dramatically in Sweden as lakes have warmed and become more eutrophic. Trophy zander over 10 kg are the ambition of thousands of Swedish anglers.

children.name: Sander lucioperca
children.description: Sweden's most prized freshwater table fish (gös). Expanding its range northward as Swedish lakes warm. Its reflective tapetum lucidum makes its eyes glow in darkness — an adaptation for hunting in murky water. The 'pike-perch' name is a misnomer; it is more closely related to perch.

children.name: Sander vitreus
children.description: The most popular sport fish in Canada and a favourite of Great Lakes anglers. Named for its opaque, glassy eyes (caused by the tapetum lucidum). Supports major commercial fisheries. State or provincial fish of many jurisdictions.

children.name: Sander marinus
children.description: A brackish-water zander of the Black and Caspian Sea basins, tolerant of saline estuarine conditions unusual for the family. Less commercially exploited than the closely related zander.

children.name: Sander volgensis
children.description: Native to the Volga, Danube and other eastern European rivers. Smaller than the zander, with which it often co-occurs, and lacking the large canine teeth of its relative.

children.name: Sander canadensis
children.description: The North American counterpart of the Volga pikeperch, native to large turbid rivers of central North America. It hybridises with walleye to produce the 'saugeye', widely stocked by anglers.

children.name: Sander oryctes
children.description: A fossil zander species from the Miocene of Europe; known from complete skeletons.

children.name: Sander africanus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Sander comatus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Sander ugandanus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Sander serratensis
children.description: A species of Sander found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Sander appalachiensis
children.description: A species of Sander found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Sander cervinus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Sander neotropicus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Sander mosambicanus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Sander contractus
children.description: A species of Sander found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Sander basalis
children.description: A species of Sander found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Gymnocephalus
children.description: Ruffe — small, spiny perch relatives of European and Asian fresh waters. The Eurasian ruffe (gers) is common but often overlooked, lurking on lake and river bottoms in turbid water where its large, sensitive lateral line helps it detect invertebrates. Ruffe introduced to the Great Lakes in ballast water in the 1980s became a significant pest, spreading rapidly and outcompeting native fish in turbid bays. The ruffe's success in North America is a lesson in the ecological consequences of ballast water introductions — small, obscure fish can cause profound disruption in novel environments.

children.name: Gymnocephalus cernua
children.description: Common in Swedish lakes and rivers but rarely targeted by anglers. A bottom-dweller of turbid, slow-flowing water. Introduced to the North American Great Lakes where it spread rapidly and is now considered highly invasive.

children.name: Gymnocephalus baloni
children.description: A Danube-basin ruffe distinguished only relatively recently from the Eurasian ruffe. It prefers deeper, slower stretches of large rivers.

children.name: Gymnocephalus schraetser
children.description: A slender, striped ruffe endemic to the Danube basin, listed in the EU Habitats Directive. Sensitive to river regulation and damming.

children.name: Gymnocephalus acerina
children.description: A small ruffe of large rivers in the Black Sea, Azov Sea, and Caspian Sea drainages of Russia and Ukraine, long treated as a subspecies of the Eurasian ruffe but now recognised as distinct on morphological and genetic grounds. It inhabits deeper, slower reaches of large rivers and is less tolerant of turbidity than its close relative.

children.name: Gymnocephalus cultratus
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Gymnocephalus cavicola
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Gymnocephalus desperatus
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Gymnocephalus saharensis
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Gymnocephalus comitatus
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Gymnocephalus conformis
children.description: A species of Gymnocephalus found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Percina
children.description: Logperch and larger darters of North America, distinguished from Etheostoma by their larger size, more elongate bodies and a more complete swim bladder. Many species are sensitive to siltation and habitat degradation.

children.name: Percina caprodes
children.description: The largest and most widespread North American darter, named for its habit of flipping stones with its snout to feed on hidden invertebrates.

children.name: Percina rex
children.description: A federally endangered darter restricted to the Roanoke River drainage of Virginia, threatened by sedimentation and impoundment.

children.name: Percina tanasi
children.description: Famous for the 1978 US Supreme Court case (TVA v. Hill) that halted the Tellico Dam under the Endangered Species Act. Feeds largely on aquatic snails in clean gravel shoals.

children.name: Percina phoxocephala
children.description: A slim darter of clear, gravel-bottomed streams across the central United States, intolerant of turbidity and siltation.

children.name: Percina aurantiaca
children.description: A large, strikingly orange darter of clear upland streams in the Tennessee River system, one of the most colourful members of the genus.

children.name: Percina shumardi
children.description: A medium-sized darter of large, sandy rivers in the Mississippi Valley and adjacent drainages, tolerant of turbidity and capable of inhabiting the main channels of major rivers where few other darters occur. Named after the physician and geologist George Candee Shumard; it is one of the few percids at home in the turbid mainstream of large lowland rivers.

children.name: Percina maculata
children.description: One of the most widespread North American darters, found in gravel and sand riffles from the Great Lakes south to the Gulf slope. The male's row of large black blotches along the flank is distinctive and conspicuous; it inhabits clean to moderately clear streams and is frequently used in studies of darter ecology.

children.name: Percina evides
children.description: A large, colourful darter of clear, swift rivers in the Ohio River basin, the breeding male suffused with gold and green. Among the more impressive-sized Percina, it favours clean, well-oxygenated riffles and is sensitive to pollution and fine sedimentation.

children.name: Percina sciera
children.description: A medium darter of sandy and gravel runs in the Ohio and Mississippi river systems, among the least demanding of the larger Percina in terms of water clarity. It is common in moderate-gradient streams with mixed sand and gravel substrate.

children.name: Percina copelandi
children.description: A small darter of large rivers from the St. Lawrence basin to the Gulf slope, one of the few darters regularly inhabiting the sandy or gravelly runs of major lowland river channels. Tolerant of moderate turbidity and current, it is a distinctive member of large-river fish communities.

children.name: Percina oxyrhyncha
children.description: A large Percina of swift, rocky streams in the New and upper Ohio River drainages, identifiable by its elongated snout and the boldly banded dorsal fin of breeding males. Characteristic of streams with fast current over bedrock and large cobble.

children.name: Percina roanoka
children.description: A small darter of rocky riffles in the Roanoke River drainage of Virginia and North Carolina, a regional endemic associated with clean, swift water over cobble and gravel substrate. It is one of several Atlantic-slope percids with restricted distributions shaped by past drainage connections.

children.name: Percina nasuta
children.description: A distinctive large darter of clear, swift Ozark streams, immediately recognisable by its exceptionally elongated snout used to probe under flat rocks for invertebrates. It is the ecological equivalent in the Ozarks of the widespread Logperch.

children.name: Percina palmaris
children.description: A subtly coloured, bronze-tinted darter of clean, fast-flowing streams in the Coosa and Tallapoosa river systems of Alabama and Georgia. It is sensitive to increased sedimentation from agricultural and urban runoff and has experienced population declines across its limited range.

children.name: Percina antesella
children.description: A critically endangered darter restricted to a short reach of the Conasauga River in Georgia and Tennessee, one of the most limited-range percids in the world. Its amber-yellow colouration and dependence on clean, fast water over gravel make it a sentinel species for river quality in the southeastern Appalachians.

children.name: Percina jenkinsi
children.description: A federally threatened logperch restricted almost entirely to the Conasauga River system in Georgia and Tennessee, one of the most imperilled members of its group. Named in honour of the American ichthyologist Robert E. Jenkins, who made major contributions to the study of southeastern US freshwater fishes.

children.name: Percina notogramma
children.description: A slender darter of rocky piedmont streams on the Atlantic slope from Pennsylvania to Virginia, associated with fast-flowing sections with cobble and gravel. It has a distinctive series of dark marks along the dorsal midline that give it its common name.

children.name: Percina gymnocephala
children.description: A medium-sized darter of fast, high-gradient streams in the New River drainage of the southern Appalachians, associated with clean, swift water over bedrock and large boulders. A species characteristic of the distinctive fish community of the ancient New River drainage.

children.name: Percina bimaculata
children.description: A logperch of the Susquehanna, Potomac, and other Chesapeake Bay drainages in Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Virginia, differing from the widespread Logperch in morphological details and distributions. It uses its overhanging snout to flip stones in search of invertebrate prey.

children.name: Percina peltata
children.description: A medium darter of rocky riffles on the Atlantic slope from New York to North Carolina, named for the shield-shaped lateral blotches along its sides. It inhabits clean, gravel-bottomed streams with good current and is sensitive to impoundment and siltation.

children.name: Percina aurora
children.description: A federally endangered darter now restricted to the Bogue Chitto River and Pearl River drainages of Mississippi and Louisiana, extirpated from much of its historical range due to channelisation, gravel dredging, and habitat degradation. One of the more imperilled darters of the Gulf Coastal Plain.

children.name: Percina aurolineata
children.description: A threatened darter of clean, swift streams in the upper Coosa River system of Alabama and Georgia, identified by the golden lateral stripe along its flank. Highly sensitive to siltation from agricultural and forestry practices, it has declined significantly with increasing land-use change in its watershed.

children.name: Percina lenticula
children.description: A large, logperch-like darter of sandy coastal plain rivers in the Gulf slope, distinguished by a freckled rather than banded pattern. Inhabiting clean, moderately swift streams with sand and gravel substrate, it uses its protruding snout to probe the bottom for invertebrates.

children.name: Percina macrocephala
children.description: A stout, large-headed darter of swift riffles in the Ohio River basin above the falls, associated with clean, fast water over cobble and bedrock substrates. Its relatively large head and robust body distinguish it from most other Percina; it has declined as river systems in the region have been degraded.

children.name: Percina nigrofasciata
children.description: A boldly patterned darter of clear, sandy-bottomed streams across the southern United States from the Gulf slope to the Carolinas, the male bearing a series of vertical black bars along the flanks. Among the more tolerant darters of the southeastern Coastal Plain, it occupies a wide range of stream sizes.

children.name: Percina notosargia
children.description: A colourful darter from the Ouachita highlands of Arkansas and Oklahoma.

children.name: Percina cynatotaenia
children.description: A blue-green darter from the Tennessee River drainage; prefers moderate current.

children.name: Percina pantolinea
children.description: A recent discovery from the Mobile Bay basin; bright turquoise breeding colours in males.

children.name: Percina nevisense
children.description: A recently described darter from the New River drainage in West Virginia.

children.name: Percina rufipes
children.description: A species of Percina found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Percina crypticus
children.description: A species of Percina found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Percina found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Percina found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Percina cuneus
children.description: A species of Percina found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Etheostoma
children.description: Darters — small, vividly coloured bottom-dwelling perch relatives endemic to North American freshwater streams. The genus is one of the most species-rich of all North American vertebrates, with many narrow-range endemics of high conservation concern.

children.name: Etheostoma caeruleum
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured North American fishes, the breeding male flushed with electric blue and orange. Inhabits clean, fast riffles of eastern US streams.

children.name: Etheostoma spectabile
children.description: A small, vividly coloured darter of small clear streams and springs in the central United States, a model species in studies of speciation and sexual selection.

children.name: Etheostoma blennioides
children.description: A relatively large darter associated with beds of aquatic moss and algae in eastern North American rivers, the breeding male suffused with green.

children.name: Etheostoma nigrum
children.description: A widespread, adaptable darter of varied habitats across eastern North America, often the most common darter in a stream. Males guard eggs deposited on the undersides of stones.

children.name: Etheostoma fonticola
children.description: A tiny endangered darter restricted to the spring-fed San Marcos and Comal rivers of Texas, dependent on constant clear, cool spring flow.

children.name: Etheostoma wapiti
children.description: A federally endangered darter of the Elk River system in Tennessee and Alabama, named for its preference for boulder-strewn riffles.

children.name: Etheostoma flabellare
children.description: A small darter of fast riffles whose males develop fleshy knobs on the dorsal fin spines used in egg care. Widespread across eastern North America.

children.name: Etheostoma olmstedi
children.description: A common darter of Atlantic slope streams from Canada to Florida, tolerant of a wide range of conditions and frequently studied in behavioural ecology.

children.name: Etheostoma rubrum
children.description: A threatened darter endemic to Bayou Pierre in Mississippi, restricted to clean gravel riffles in a single small drainage.

children.name: Etheostoma sellare
children.description: Possibly extinct, this darter was known only from a few sites in Maryland and has not been reliably seen since the late 1980s, making it one of the most imperilled North American fishes.

children.name: Etheostoma exile
children.description: A small, adaptable darter of weedy margins of lakes and slow-moving streams across central and eastern North America, tolerant of lower water quality than most darters. Breeding males develop vivid red and green markings; it is among the few darters regularly found in standing or near-standing water.

children.name: Etheostoma zonale
children.description: A widespread and attractive darter of clean, fast riffles in eastern North American streams, the male banded with green and olive bars across the flanks. Among the more pollution-tolerant darters, it often persists in streams subject to moderate disturbance and is frequently encountered in riffle-pool sequences.

children.name: Etheostoma cinereum
children.description: A threatened darter of large, clear rivers in the Tennessee River basin, preferring swift current over bedrock or large boulders. Named for its ash-grey colouration in females and non-breeding males; one of the larger and more impressive species in the genus.

children.name: Etheostoma tippecanoe
children.description: An endangered darter restricted to clean gravel riffles in the Wabash River system of Indiana and Ohio, one of the rarest members of the genus. Highly sensitive to siltation and agricultural runoff, it has disappeared from most of its historical range as the Wabash watershed has been intensively farmed.

children.name: Etheostoma microperca
children.description: The smallest darter and one of the smallest North American vertebrates, rarely exceeding 35 mm in length. Found in weedy margins of lakes and quiet backwaters, it hangs motionless among aquatic plants and ambushes small invertebrates rather than darting across the substrate like most relatives.

children.name: Etheostoma histrio
children.description: A boldly patterned darter of large, sandy rivers with extensive beds of water willow in the southeastern United States. The breeding male is one of the most elaborately marked darters, with contrasting red and green hues; it lays eggs among the stems of water willow.

children.name: Etheostoma variatum
children.description: A large, variable darter of riffle zones in Ohio River tributaries, the male developing rich red and orange pigments in breeding condition. Among the first darters studied intensively for spawning behaviour; it buries eggs under gravel in swift water.

children.name: Etheostoma stigmaeum
children.description: A small, heavily speckled darter of gravel runs and riffles across much of the southeastern United States, tolerant of moderate turbidity. One of the commoner darters across the Gulf slope and lower Mississippi drainage, often co-occurring with more specialised species.

children.name: Etheostoma simoterum
children.description: A small darter of upland streams in the Cumberland Plateau and adjacent ridges, named for its distinctly short, blunt snout that separates it from related species in the same drainage. It inhabits gravel-bottomed riffles and runs with clear water.

children.name: Etheostoma maculatum
children.description: A state-threatened darter of clean, fast riffles in the Ohio River basin, sensitive to fine sedimentation and characteristic of streams with excellent water quality. The male bears a row of red spots along the flank that are diagnostic in breeding condition.

children.name: Etheostoma podostemone
children.description: A specialist darter of Virginia and West Virginia mountain streams, living almost exclusively on beds of riverweed (Podostemum ceratophyllum), where it forages for invertebrates and deposits eggs. The dense mats of riverweed it requires are themselves indicators of clean, fast, sunlit water.

children.name: Etheostoma sagitta
children.description: A brightly coloured darter of upland streams in southeastern Kentucky and adjacent Tennessee, one of the showier members of the genus with orange and blue breeding males. A focal species of conservation efforts in the upper Cumberland River system.

children.name: Etheostoma parvipinne
children.description: A small, gold-striped darter of sandy-bottomed lowland streams across the southeastern United States, found in sluggish, warm water with abundant aquatic vegetation. It is among the more tolerant darters of low-gradient coastal plain streams.

children.name: Etheostoma tuscumbia
children.description: A critically endangered darter restricted to a handful of spring-fed streams in Colbert County, Alabama, dependent on stable, cold, clear spring water. One of the most geographically limited percids in North America, it is vulnerable to groundwater abstraction and any alterations to its spring sources.

children.name: Etheostoma virgatum
children.description: A well-marked darter of clean gravel riffles in the Cumberland and Tennessee River systems, the male bearing strong horizontal stripes along the flank that are diagnostic. It favours swift, well-oxygenated water with a cobble and gravel substrate.

children.name: Etheostoma chlorobranchium
children.description: A strikingly coloured darter of fast, cool mountain streams in the New and Kanawha river drainages of Appalachia, with vivid green dorsal and pectoral fins that earned it its common name. It is one of the more localised members of the genus, associated with high-quality upland streams.

children.name: Etheostoma pyrrhogaster
children.description: A small darter of clean gravel riffles in the Cumberland River system, the male developing a fiery orange-red belly in breeding condition that ranks among the most vivid colourations of any darter species. Its distribution is limited to upland tributaries of the Cumberland plateau.

children.name: Etheostoma camurum
children.description: A large, brilliantly coloured darter of riffles in Ohio River tributaries, the breeding male developing vivid blue and orange breast patches that make it one of the most spectacular stream fishes in North America. It inhabits clean, fast riffles with coarse gravel or bedrock and is sensitive to fine sediment deposition.

children.name: Etheostoma swannanoa
children.description: A darter of fast, rocky streams in the French Broad River system of the southern Appalachians, restricted to a relatively limited area and sensitive to fine sediment. It is characteristic of high-gradient mountain streams with clean, boulder-strewn riffles.

children.name: Etheostoma kennicotti
children.description: A small, common darter of clear upland streams across the Cumberland Plateau, with a distinctive series of horizontal stripes at the base of the caudal fin. Named for the American naturalist Robert Kennicott; it is frequently abundant in streams of appropriate quality in Kentucky and Tennessee.

children.name: Etheostoma squamiceps
children.description: A medium-sized darter of moderate to fast riffles in the Green River system of Kentucky and Tennessee, recognisable by a dark spot at the base of the caudal fin. Males guard nests under flat stones and exhibit biparental care behaviour studied in stream ecology research.

children.name: Etheostoma gracile
children.description: A small, adaptable darter of lowland sloughs, sluggish streams, and shallow lakes across the central and southeastern United States. Among the most tolerant darters of standing or slow-moving, turbid water, it fills a niche distinct from the riffle-dwelling majority of the genus.

children.name: Etheostoma proeliare
children.description: A minute darter of swampy lowland forest streams and cypress sloughs across the Gulf Coastal Plain, among the smallest members of the genus. It inhabits habitats with dense aquatic vegetation and little flow, often resting motionless on plant stems.

children.name: Etheostoma ditrema
children.description: A critically endangered darter restricted to a handful of spring-fed streams in Etowah County, Alabama, where it depends on stable, cold, clear spring water year-round. Its extreme dependence on spring flow makes it one of the most vulnerable of all darters to groundwater abstraction and climate-driven hydrological change.

children.name: Etheostoma lachneri
children.description: A small species endemic to the Cahaba River in Alabama; named after the ichthyologist.

children.name: Etheostoma pallididorsum
children.description: A beautiful darter from the Ouachita Mountains; pale back contrasts with dark flank bars.

children.name: Etheostoma corradoi
children.description: A recently described species from the Duck River system in Tennessee.

children.name: Etheostoma chermocki
children.description: A brilliantly coloured darter from the Mobile Basin.

children.name: Etheostoma boschungi
children.description: A threatened species from the Tennessee Valley; breeds in seasonally flooded fields.

children.description: A species of Etheostoma found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.description: A species of Etheostoma found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Zingel
children.description: Streber and zingel — elongate, bottom-dwelling perch relatives of the Danube and other central European river systems, adapted to fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water.

children.name: Zingel zingel
children.description: An elongate, bottom-dwelling perch relative of the Danube and Dniester basins, listed under the EU Habitats Directive and sensitive to river regulation.

children.name: Zingel streber
children.description: A slender Danubian perch relative of fast-flowing gravel reaches, threatened by damming and channelisation across central Europe.

children.name: Zingel asper
children.description: A critically endangered perch relative endemic to the Rhône basin in France, reduced to a few fragmented populations by dams and pollution.

children.name: Zingel balcanicus
children.description: A slender, elongate percid endemic to rivers draining into Lake Ohrid and the southern Vardar system in North Macedonia, Albania, and Greece, listed as vulnerable. It requires clean, swift water over gravel beds and is threatened by water extraction, pollution, and hydropower development in its fragmented range.

children.name: Zingel clavicornis
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Zingel aequatorialis
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Zingel auratus
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Zingel chlorocephalus
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Zingel nigripes
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Zingel dignus
children.description: A species of Zingel found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Romanichthys
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the asprete, one of the most endangered fish in Europe, restricted to a few rivers in Romania.

children.name: Romanichthys valsanicola
children.description: One of Europe's most endangered fish and a living fossil, surviving only in a short stretch of the Vâlsan River in Romania, with a wild population numbering in the tens.

children.name: Crystallaria
children.description: Crystal darters — a monotypic genus of nearly transparent, sand-burrowing darters of the Mississippi River system. The Crystal Darter is adapted for a semi-fossorial lifestyle in clean, shifting sand substrate, a niche distinct from the rock-riffle habitats favoured by most Percidae.

children.name: Crystallaria asprella
children.description: A nearly transparent darter of clean, sandy-bottomed rivers in the Mississippi River system, adapted for a semi-burrowing lifestyle in shifting sand. It lacks the brilliant colours of most darters but compensates with remarkable translucency that provides camouflage against a sandy background; it buries itself with only its eyes above the surface.

children.name: Crystallaria cincta
children.description: A rare and translucent darter from the Green and Cumberland rivers; federally endangered.

children.name: Ammocrypta
children.description: Sand darters — small, slender, nearly translucent darters specialised for burrowing in clean, sandy river bottoms across eastern North America. They bury themselves with only the eyes exposed, ambushing invertebrate prey and escaping predators. Several species are endangered due to siltation of their sand-substrate habitats.

children.name: Ammocrypta pellucida
children.description: A slender, nearly transparent sand-burrowing darter of the Great Lakes region and St. Lawrence drainage, endangered in Canada and threatened across much of its US range by siltation of sandy river beds. It buries itself in clean sand with only its eyes protruding, and its translucent body provides effective camouflage against the substrate.

children.name: Ammocrypta vivax
children.description: A pale, sand-burrowing darter of the Gulf Coastal Plain from Mississippi east to the Choctawhatchee drainage in Florida, inhabiting sandy runs and shoals in clear, moderately sized streams. More heavily scaled than the Eastern Sand Darter, a feature reflected in its common name.

children.name: Ammocrypta clara
children.description: A sand-burrowing darter of the Mississippi River valley from Illinois and Iowa south to Louisiana, less studied than its eastern relative but equally dependent on clean, shifting sand substrates for feeding and escape from predation. Agricultural siltation has reduced its abundance across much of its historical range.

children.name: Ammocrypta beani
children.description: A Gulf Coast species; almost completely translucent body; buries into sand when startled.

children.name: Ammocrypta bifascia
children.description: Endemic to Florida's panhandle; the only Ammocrypta in the Florida peninsula.

children.name: Ammocrypta borealis
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ammocrypta crenulatus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ammocrypta chrysostomus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ammocrypta sanguineus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ammocrypta chrysopygus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Asia, Atlantic, Africa, Pacific, Indian, Europe, North America, South America and Australia.

children.name: Ammocrypta celebensis
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Ammocrypta peruanus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Ammocrypta shuar
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Ammocrypta brevicaudatus
children.description: A species of Ammocrypta found across Pacific, Indian, Australia, Atlantic, North America, Africa, Europe, Asia and South America.

children.name: Hadropterus
children.description: A group of larger darters with a blunt conical snout; inhabit swift streams.

children.name: Hadropterus nigrofasciatus
children.description: A medium-sized darter from the Santee and Savannah river drainages; bold black bars on flanks.

children.name: Hadropterus scienius
children.description: Found in the Ouachita and Arkansas River highlands; large head with blunt snout.

children.name: Hadropterus uranoscopium
children.description: Named for its upward-facing eyes; inhabits deep riffles of Tennessee River tributaries.

children.name: Catopharynx
children.description: A genus of small bottom-dwelling percids found in eastern North American streams.

children.name: Catopharynx labeo
children.description: Endemic to the Cayuga Lake basin in New York; one of the northernmost darters.

children.name: Catopharynx vitreus
children.description: A semi-transparent darter from the Tennessee Valley; spawning occurs in gravel nests.
